data.title: BLOX: A Weekly Paying ETF That Has The Potential To Capture Bitcoin Growth
data.description:
Overview

With the rise of high yield ETFs, I've stayed away from any of the crypto related funds since Bitcoin (BTC-USD) was hovering near its all-time highs. Now that crypto has pulled back more than 20% from its highs, funds like the Nicholas Crypto Income ETF (BLOX) have become a bit more enticing. However, I believe there are a few caveats that investors must consider before initiating a position in this weekly paying ETF. Although I believe that BLOX may present less risk than some alternatives, I still think that BLOX should be considered a high risk/high reward fund.

BLOX has an inception dating back to June of 2025, meaning that the fund hasn't been time tested through different market cycles yet. BLOX takes an actively managed approach and gives investors the opportunity to get simultaneous exposure to both Bitcoin and Ethereum (ETH-USD), while also placing an emphasis on weekly distributions. BLOX also has a reasonable expense ratio of 1.03%, which is fair considering the high distribution rate of 36% that is being paid out to investors.

When compared against one of the most notable peers in the high yield crypto space, I believe that BLOX's more conservative approach to its distribution may be the more suitable choice during a market downturn. The crypto market's momentum seems to be shifting, so the risk of a greater downside movement remains present. Despite the fund's short history, BLOX has done a great job at keeping payouts consistent so far. So let's start by taking a look at the underlying strategy that BLOX implements to generate its earnings.

Fund Strategy

According to the latest fact sheet, BLOX has total assets under management of $198.7M that is spread across 70 different positions. The fund seeks to generate income primarily using option contracts on some or all of the holdings of the equity and crypto portfolio. Unlike some peers in the sector, BLOX differentiates itself because it actually holds the equities that it writes options against, which reduces the volatility and risk of capital deterioration over time. The fund allocates its assets into a blend of digital assets, as well as companies engaged in the different sub-categories of the crypto industry. This includes segments such as:

  1. Crypto Asset Mining
  2. Blockchain Technology
  3. Crypto Trading Platforms
  4. Payment Processing
  5. DeFi Technology Providers

When looking at the holdings below, we can see that BLOX is heavily allocated to the VanEck Bitcoin ETF (HODL) at 14.85% of assets, which directly holds Bitcoin within, as supported by its overview. This is followed by other notable holdings such as Hut 8 Corp (HUT), IREN (IREN), Robinhood Markets (HOOD), and Galaxy Digital (GLXY), just to name a few. In short, all of these businesses are exposed to the crypto industry in some capacity. The clear risk comes from the fact that these are companies that are typically more volatile and tied to the momentum of the crypto markets, more specifically, Bitcoin.

In order to capitalize on this volatility, BLOX writes options against a portion of its holdings, which helps to generate the income needed to support the large dividend yield. BLOX essentially turns this volatility into option premiums, which are used to fuel the distributions that are paid out to shareholders. This means that BLOX can offer massive distributions but it can also come at the cost of capital appreciation. Simply put, BLOX's options will limit the price growth to whatever the selected strike price is.

Performance Risk

Although the fund's operating history is limited, the volatile market environment has already demonstrated the fund's ability to participate in the momentum of the market and its holdings. As we can see below, BLOX's share price has marginally increased by 0.84% since its inception. However, there was a point where BLOX's share price was approaching a 50% gain only a month ago. So during a crypto bull market, BLOX can be a solid position to partially participate in the positive momentum. When including all distributions paid, the total return jumps up to 10.8% over the same time frame.

However, the returns are a bit skewed at the moment, considering that Bitcoin has experienced a sharp pullback from its highs. As we can see below, BLOX's share price will stay closely tied to the momentum and price movement of BTC, which makes sense considering that all of BLOX's holdings also share a similar price movement to BTC. While the price of BTC did pullback from its highs, the risk remains that the price can always fall further.

While there are other peers that implement a similar option writing strategy against a portfolio of companies with exposure to crypto, I believe that YieldMax Crypto Industry & Tech Portfolio Option Income ETF (LFGY) is the most compelling peer. While the underlying holdings and strategy when it comes to its option writing strategy may be different, the underlying premise of trying to turn the sector's volatility into income remains similar. LFGY now offers investors a starting dividend yield of ~70.7% and issues those payouts on a weekly basis. I recently published an article on LFGY that highlights the fund's strategy and risk profile that I'd recommend reading if you are interested in a more dedicated analysis.

As we can see below, LFGY shares a similar level of assets under management at $181M, while also having a slightly lower expense ratio of 0.99%. Additionally, LFGY has a slightly longer operating history that dates back to January of 2025. While the two funds share similar fundamentals, I think the key difference comes down to the yield and the use case, based on market conditions. LFGY's large distribution yield is more beneficial during periods where BTC and the overall crypto market is trending upwards. Conversely, LFGY may not be the better choice during pullbacks or flat markets, because the large distributions will have a negative impact on the NAV and capital deterioration.

I believe that BLOX is the more suitable choice for investors that want a large yield but have a lower risk tolerance for massive share price erosion. BLOX's more conservative yield helps the fund reduce how severe the downside can be in comparison to LFGY. To help demonstrate the differences, I pulled in a performance chart between the two options since BLOX's inception.

As we can see below, BLOX's share price has declined by about 8.45% since its inception, compared to LFGY's more drastic decline of 31.67% over the same period. Despite BLOX's having a lower distribution rate, the fund's total return is also higher than LFGY's over the same period. So if the crypto markets will remain choppy in the near-term, BLOX may be the better choice.

Dividends

As of the latest declared weekly payout of $0.1529 per share, the current dividend yield sits around 36%. When we're talking about a yield this large, there are a lot of variables that must be considered. For instance, payouts are determined by the success of the written options, momentum of the market/crypto, and the direction of BLOX's underlying holdings. Therefore, the declared distribution is likely to be different week to week. As confirmed by the dividend history below, we can see that payouts vary every week.

However, BLOX has done a great job at keeping the payouts within a consistent range, while avoiding any major swings in payouts. I believe that this consistency should be commended because it allows investors to create their own dividend growth by reinvesting portions of their dividends or by adding more capital over time. However, this is only valid assuming that payouts maintain this steady range over the next twelve months. Therefore, the risk remains that the payout amounts can see a large decline if the crypto markets pullback further.

Since the yield is so large, this enables patient investors to reach 'house money' status when holding for a long period of time. Some quick math tells us that investors can receive their initial investment back within a three year period, assuming that payouts do not experience a large decline. To demonstrate this, we can use a simple example with a $10,000 investment:

  • $10,000 invested at a 36% dividend yield = ~$3,600 in annual income.
  • $10,000 / ~$3,600 annual income = 2.77 years until house money status.

Of course, this requires BLOX to keep payouts the same which may not be a realistic expectation in a crypto downturn. I just wanted to use this as an example to demonstrate the power of a large distributions during more favorable market conditions. So if you are bullish on the outlook of crypto, a fund like BLOX can be a great addition to an income portfolio. Once you receive your initial investment back in distributions, your overall risk level is drastically reduced from that point forward.

Lastly, the fund tends to utilize return of capital distributions in an effort to reduce the tax impact. For instance, the latest section19(a) notice indicates that 68.6% of the payouts YTD were fueled by return of capital distributions. Return of capital distributions aren't classified as income and therefore, aren't taxed as such. Instead, a return of capital distribution reduces an investor's cost basis and allows taxes to be deferred until the time of sale. However, there will still be some tax consequences to consider since the remaining portion of payouts are fueled by net investment income. Therefore, a position in BLOX may be best utilized within a tax-advantaged account.

Takeaway

In conclusion, I believe that BLOX is a compelling buy for investors that want to harness the volatility of the crypto markets. Now that Bitcoin has pulled back more than 20% from its all-time high, it may be a good time to begin accumulating BLOX. The fund's portfolio of holdings may have risks associated with the volatility of crypto, but the lower yield and consistent payouts makes BLOX a better choice than LFGY. The fund has displayed consistent payouts since its inception so far, while also demonstrating that the share price can participate in some price growth in a scenario where crypto eventually heads higher.

data.title: Cipher: The Silent Winner Of AI Infra
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Cipher Mining (CIFR) has silently moved past a point that most investors behind AI infrastructure investments never get to see, as its contracted AI hosting revenue now exceeds its entire market value. This itself should have prompted a rerating in the market.

Nvidia's (NVDA) latest earnings only reinforce the setup: AI data center demand isn't slowing, but it's compounding, with power and energized capacity emerging as the real bottlenecks. Sitting on $8.5 billion in binding AWS and Google/Fluidstack leases, Cipher monetizes megawatts faster than peers, yet trades as if none of this future exists. That mispricing is the opportunity.

I initiated a position in Cipher before Nvidia's earnings, fully expecting the data center demand backdrop to validate the entire neocloud thesis.

The Revenue That Should Have Repriced the Stock

The most important thing to understand about Cipher now is simple: the company is sitting on more contracted revenue than its enterprise value. That is extremely rare for a hyperscaler-grade infrastructure developer. With roughly $6 billion in EV and more than $8.5 billion in cumulative contracted hosting revenue already signed, Cipher has crossed a threshold that typically forces a valuation reset. This is real revenue, backed by executed leases, and tied directly to AI workloads that hyperscalers need energized as quickly as possible.

The $5.5 billion AWS agreement alone should have turned heads. It's a 15-year lease with the world's largest cloud provider, structured around an accelerated deployment at Black Pearl, where existing infrastructure can be upgraded into liquid- and air-cooled HPC capacity. On top of that sits the Fluidstack and Google deal, which carries roughly $3 billion of contracted value, backed by a $1.4 billion Google guarantee. These are not soft commitments. They are contractual obligations that hyperscalers have intentionally secured ahead of a tightening power landscape.

AI-infrastructure peers with signed MoUs, not even revenue-bearing leases, have rallied on far weaker news. Cipher has actual binding revenue commitments, yet still trades at levels that imply none of this is fully priced in.

What Nvidia's Blowout Earnings Really Mean for Neoclouds, and Why CIFR Sits in the Slipstream

One thing is very obvious from Nvidia's recent earnings: the AI infrastructure market is not slowing down but accelerating at a pace beyond even optimists' expectations. Data center sales broke new ground with yet another record; hyperscalers' shelves are empty from generation to generation of GPUs, while demand is accumulating for all types of activities, including training tasks, post-training tasks, and inferencing tasks. What really mattered was not data points but Jensen's message that the limit is no longer GPU but power and energizable capacities.

This alone gives a huge value shift to the neoclouds. Hyperscalers just can't churn fast enough because wait times for grid access are measured in years, transformers also take years to deliver, and permitting is slow. Therefore, inferentially, the AI expansion necessarily leaks into whoever can energize power the fastest.

The capability of CIFR is no longer theoretical at this point. It is entering an industry where, mathematically speaking, the need will evidently outpace what hyperscalers can deliver. Therefore, CIFR is naturally poised to benefit from Nvidia's multi-year cycle of growth.

A Monetization Timeline That Pulls the Earnings Curve Forward

Part of the mismatch stems from timing. Cipher is not building its 300-megawatt AWS deployment from scratch. The Black Pearl site already has 150 megawatts constructed and operating as a high-end mining facility. This will help deliver Phase 1 of the AWS project by mid-2026, months or even one full year before hyperscalers' normal cycles for deployment. Then comes Phase 2, scheduled to happen by the latter half of 2026.

The direct financial implication is that Cipher's revenue recognition pulls forward. EPS doesn't start in 2027 or 2028; it accelerates in 2026. This matters because the market is unusually sensitive to timing in the current AI buildout. Hyperscalers are desperate for immediate power and compute, and companies that can deliver earlier inevitably command better economics and higher valuation multiples.

The Fluidstack and Google site at Barber Lake follows a similar pattern. Construction is already underway. Long-lead items are secured. The site is on track for full delivery in September 2026. When you layer both projects on top of one another, Cipher's entire operating model begins monetizing faster than analysts originally expected. And that's before we even talk about the 1 GW Colchis project energizing in 2028, one of the largest long-duration power agreements in the pipeline of any AI-centered infrastructure company.

Reading Between the Lines of Current Valuation Metrics

Analyst expectations show revenue of $253.6 million in 2025 and $419.8 million in 2026, with forward EPS estimates of $0.34 and $0.49, respectively. But those numbers do not yet incorporate the AWS or Fluidstack-Google leases, because neither will begin contributing until the back half of 2026.

CIFR's EV of $5.8 billion also looks challenging at first glance but is certainly undervalued considering their contract agreements. Their contracted capacities for AI-related markets include 544 MW of fully contracted AI capacity, 300 MW contracted with AWS, and 244 MW contracted with Google/FluidStack, adding around $200 million of lease revenue each year upon entering commercial operation.

Since all these leases come into effect in the latter half of 2026, my projections for Cipher are at least hitting $350-400 million for 2026 alone and do not include new contract business or any HPC conversion opportunities based on 3.2 GW of development portfolio.

On a forward-looking valuation, this puts CIFR into the 13x EV/2026 revenue multiple, which is rich for the average miner but not for hyperscaler-grade infra lessors with 15-year agreements, zero-coupon bonds, and some of the fastest times to megawatts in the market. If 2027 revenues hit expectations in the AWS+Google deal at $700 million+, its EV/R multiple dips into the sub-8x range, indicating substantial upside.

When Cipher issued its $1.3 billion convertible notes, institutions received the right to convert that debt into shares at $14.5. Cipher valued the embedded conversion option at $414 million, which effectively reveals what those investors believe the stock will be worth later. Once you divide that option value by the number of potential new shares, you get roughly $5/share of implied premium above the strike price, putting the long-term expectation around $21-$22/share, consistent with the capped-call protection that extends the economic ceiling to $23.32/share.

Discounted back to today, that future range lands in the $8-$10/share zone, showing that institutions were willing to accept a 0% coupon because they expect significant multi-year upside in CIFR's AI hosting business. Based on contracted Phase 1 revenues alone, plus the valuation implied by the $1.3 billion convertible notes, a conservative price target sits around $20-$25 for 2027. At today's ~$14.5, the stock still trades below its contracted earnings power.

Cipher's Biggest Threat

By far, Cipher's biggest risk is its hyperscaler projects, especially the retrofit to AWS on its Black Pearl site, now called 'Black Pearl AWS's site,' and its new Google/FluidStack site on 'Barber Lake.' These are major projects involving multiple phases for HPC, with stringent energization deadlines: AWS in August 2026 and Google in October 2026. Anything hindering these projects in construction, by transforming capacity, supply chain, or permitting, shortens overall revenue timing, substantially impacting their bottom line.

Takeaway

Cipher is no longer a mining company experimenting with HPC. It is an AI-infrastructure developer with hyperscaler-backed revenue, accelerated monetization, and nondilutive capital, yet its valuation still reflects the past rather than the future. In my view, that dislocation won't last.

data.title: Japan's New Fiscal Stimulus To Target Inflation As JGB Concerns Mount
data.description:
By Min Joo Kang, Senior Economist, South Korea and Japan

Larger-than-expected fiscal stimulus package introduced

The Japanese government has approved a ¥21.3 trillion economic package (3.5% of GDP), allocating ¥11.7tr to inflation control and childcare support. Since ¥17.7tr will be drawn from the general account spending, an extra budget will probably be needed. The market anticipates that another ¥7tr in Japanese government bonds (JGBs) will be issued. Because this government is a minority government, the final package ended up larger than initially intended in order to meet opposition parties' demands, such as abolishing the gasoline tax and raising the income tax-free threshold.

The subsidies for gas and power bills for the first three months of 2026 are expected to push down the overall inflation gauge by an average of 0.7ppt during the period, while cash payouts and aid to revitalise the local/rural economy are expected to boost GDP growth by 1.4ppt per year over the next three years.

Economic package will boost near-term growth while curbing headline inflation significantly

Should the package gain approval in parliament with backing from opposition parties, it may result in a significant increase in GDP during the first half of 2026. We have to monitor how the final package is shaped, but given the considerable size of the bill, it will likely boost near-term growth. In turn, we now expect GDP to grow 1.4% year-on-year in 2026 (vs. 0.7% currently).

For inflation, the energy subsidy will lower headline inflation quite meaningfully during the wintertime, although it should be partially offset by the weaker yen. Headline inflation is expected to fall below 2% throughout next year. However, core-core inflation excluding food and energy is likely to remain above 2% for a considerable time.

Reflecting on the inflation measures, we have revised down our CPI outlook for 2026. We now expect headline inflation to rise 2.0% YoY (vs. the current 2.3%), and core-core inflation to increase 2.6% (vs. 2.7% currently) as the stimulus package strengthens demand-side pressures and keeps the JPY under pressure.

Pressures on JGB will continue

The yield on the 10-year JGB rose to around 1.8%, reflecting investor expectations of additional bond issuance. The current political environment may also limit the pace of the Bank of Japan's monetary policy normalisation, potentially allowing elevated core inflation to persist.

We believe that the pressures on the JGB will continue to build until it reaches 2.0%. This will mostly justify sustainable inflation growth of 2.0%, in our view. We've therefore revised our JGB outlook from the current 1.75% to 1.90% for the first quarter of 2026 and from 1.8% to 2.0% for the year's second half.

BoJ watch

We maintain the view that the Bank of Japan will continue on its path toward policy normalisation, with a total of 50bp in rate hikes by the end of 2026. However, the exact timing remains uncertain. Elevated inflation and a robust economic recovery could prompt the BoJ to implement a rate hike as early as December.

Still, if politics influence monetary policy, then the BoJ may postpone action until next January - or beyond, possibly until March or April, depending on the spring wage negotiation results. We will see a few major data releases next week, including Tokyo CPI and monthly industrial production, so we will adjust our BoJ view according to the data and other economic developments.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more

Original Post

data.title: Scanning For Well-Priced Yield In Fixed Income - FGSN
data.description:
With market valuations for equities and interest rates stubbornly high, I have been looking for good value yields in fixed income. I have recently found some good candidates in the insurance space, which is a segment that I know well.

You can see my article on KMPB here and RZB here. In both cases I found what I consider to be safe investments with yields exceeding 7%.

Another example, F&G Annuities & Life, Inc. CAL 65 (FGSN) from F&G Annuities & Life, Inc. (FG), caught my eye.

F&G Annuities & Life CAL 65 Details

FGSN is a January 2025-issued Junior Subordinated Note with a 7.35% yield, maturing in 2065. It is quite common for life insurance companies to have very long-dated bonds, as they need to match the duration of their insurance liabilities with their investment revenues.

As this is the bottom tier of bonds from F&G, FGSN was credit-rated BB by S&P, BB by Fitch, and Ba1 by Moody's. Here is the Fitch rating snapshot.

From the prospectus excerpt below, you can see that it is a relatively small offering of $375 million and is callable in 2030. The rate is fixed for the duration.

The pricing of the bond has fallen since the issuance date, and it now trades at $23.30, offering a 7.8% yield.

This fits the sweet spot of my target of finding strong-yielding fixed-income opportunities with well-capitalized insurance companies. The next step is a look at the sponsor.

F&G Annuities & Life

F&G Annuities & Life, Inc., a subsidiary of Fidelity National Financial, Inc., issues annuities and life insurance products in the United States. F&G was incorporated in Des Moines, Iowa, way back in 1959.

The company's products include life insurance, fixed and variable annuities, guaranteed annuities for retail customers, and pension risk transfer solutions for corporations.

This is a similar product mix to the annuity business managed by specialists such as Brookfield Wealth Solutions (BNT) and Legal & General (OTCPK:LGGNY), as well as Reinsurance Group of America (RGA), all of whom I have covered in the past.

The main risks that this segment holds include mortality and longevity risk, interest rate, and spread risk. The broader the product offered in terms of annuity guarantees, the riskier the business is.

Offsetting this is the ability of the company to invest in assets that allow a spread over the benefits paid out to members.

The company enjoys strong ratings, with S&P Global, for example, rating the company as A-, as detailed in its Q3 investor update.

Capitalization is strong, with debt to capital close to the 25% long-term target.

And I like the fact that a company this old can show consistent positive rating momentum.

What I like less is the invested asset mix. Only 29% is corporate debt, with large allocations to structured credit, primarily CLOs, private credit, and mortgages.

This sits at what I like to call the "racy side" of asset allocation for an insurance company.

Compare this, for example, to Reinsurance Group of America, the sponsor of bond RZB mentioned above, which has over 70% in investment-grade bonds. While investors in the common stock could argue the merits of more conservative investment profiles, for fixed income, in my view, the lower the risk of the investment assets of an insurer, the safer their bonds.

And F&G has not had great recent performance. Adjusted net earnings have fallen year on year as of Q3.

The explanation of this makes a good case study. For the last 5 quarters, the investment income from alternative assets has fallen below the aggressive 10% return hurdle set by management.

Conclusion

F&G is a well-rated sponsor, as with many insurance companies that need to be well capitalized in order to offer the long-term commitments that they make to pay claims for their policyholders.

Examination of the F&G assets indicates that the asset mix is somewhat riskier than peers. The results of the company have been deteriorating, with the key driver being underperformance of the alternative (higher yielding) assets.

While FSGN yields 7.8% at current prices, the bond falls in my target zone when looking for safe and well-priced yields.

However, considering the long duration and relatively low rating of FGSN, there are, in my view, better alternatives that generate equivalent or even higher yields.

I rate FGSN a Sell.

data.title: Zurich Insurance: Income Remains The Most Attractive Feature Of Its Investment Case
data.description:
As I’ve covered in the past, I see Zurich Insurance (OTCQX:ZURVY)(OTCQX:ZFSVF) as a quality company in the European insurance sector with an attractive dividend yield, but I saw upside potential as somewhat limited due to its high valuation some months ago. Not surprisingly, since my last article, its shares have traded sideways and have underperformed the overall stock market by some margin, given that its total return was only 1.5% since last May compared to close to 13% for the S&P 500 index (SPY).

This underwhelming performance seems to be justified by its shares trading at a premium valuation, as the operational performance has remained somewhat positive in recent quarters.

Indeed, during the first half of 2025 (H1 2025) its gross premiums written increased by 10% YoY, a very good performance that was supported both by its life and Property & Casualty (P&C) segments. The Business Operating Profit (BOP) amounted to $4.2 billion, up by 5% YoY, with the P&C segment reaching a new operating profit record of $2.4 billion in the semester.

These results were justified by a better underwriting result, as the combined ratio improved by 120 basis points (bps) to 92.4% in H1 2025, supported by higher premiums and lower claims costs as the company maintained strict underwriting criteria. In the Farmers unit, operating profit also reached a new record for the first half of the year, a very good result considering the significant losses related to L.A. wildfires at the beginning of the year, that were nonetheless manageable as I was expecting some months ago.

On the other hand, its investment result declined by 18% YoY to $653 million, justified by lower capital gains and higher insurance and reinsurance finance expenses. However, Zurich has a lower reliance on investment results than other insurance companies, like Allianz (OTCPK:ALIZY) that has much lower underwriting results and most of its earnings come from investments, making its bottom-line much more exposed to underwriting profitability rather than investment results.

Despite its good operational performance in H1 2025, its net profit increased by only 1.3% YoY to $3.06 billion, pressured by lower investment returns, restructuring costs, and other adjustments. This shows that Zurich’s bottom-line growth is not fantastic, which is not surprising considering that the company is already quite large and operates in a mature industry.

Related to the third quarter of 2025, Zurich only reported a trading update some weeks ago, as it usually does on a quarterly basis while earnings are reported on a half-year and annual basis. During the first nine months of 2025 (9M 2025), its gross premiums written increased by 8% YoY to $38.9 billion in the P&C segment, supported by its retail and commercial insurance lines, while life insurance premiums were up by 11% supported by the sale of capital-efficient savings and protection products. In the U.S., Farmers Exchanges reported an increase of 5% in gross written premiums, maintaining a very good operating performance supported by policy growth and positive pricing.

Regarding its capital position, Zurich’s Swiss Solvency Test (SST) ratio was 257% at the end of last September, a very good ratio and well above its internal target of at least 160%. Therefore, Zurich remains very well capitalized and does not have much need to retain earnings in the near future, being in a position of strength to maintain an interesting capital returns strategy ahead.

A couple of days ago, Zurich performed an investor day, updating the group’s strategy to investors and analysts. There weren’t many surprises as the company’s strategy remains focused on improving its underwriting profitability and growing organically its several business segments. Regarding its main financial targets, Zurich continues to target EPS growth of high-single digits, achieve a core return on equity (ROE) ratio above 23%, and return more than $19 billion of cash remittances to the holding company during the period 2025-27.

This means the company’s financial profile is not expected to change much over the coming years, as Zurich is a large company operating in a mature industry, which has somewhat muted growth prospects. Despite that, there are some areas where the company can achieve higher growth, especially related to specialty insurance lines.

These are lines that are not covered by standard commercial policies, such as cyber risk, construction, energy, or financial lines. These lines include coverage tailored to a specific industry or specialized exposures, being an area that requires more specific knowledge and capacity to properly price risk than standard lines, leading to less competition than compared to traditional insurance coverage.

While Zurich already has a good presence in specialty insurance, it’s not a leading player in this segment, which is dominated by U.S. companies, such as Fidelity National Financial (FNF) or Assurant (AIZ). Nevertheless, global growth themes like the investments related to the construction of data centers is an opportunity for specialty insurance companies to grow insurance premiums, of which Zurich is in good position to grab market share by being a leader in this sector in the U.S. Indeed, close to one-third of gross premiums written by Zurich in the specialty segment were related to construction & engineering during 2024, showing that it has developed internal expertise to take risks profitably in this area boding well for growth ahead.

While Zurich’s growth prospects aren’t particularly impressive, there are some segments where the company can increase its business, including specialty lines, the middle market, and the retail segment. Indeed, Zurich expects insurance growth, portfolio optimization and better efficiency to be the main drivers of earnings growth over the next couple of years, while investment results should be a headwind due to lower interest rates expected compared to recent years.

Given this background, it’s not surprising to see that current consensus estimates show Zurich’s revenues increasing to about $67 billion by 2027 (vs. $61 billion expected in 2025) and its net profit should be above $7.3 billion, compared to $6.5 billion this year. These estimates are not very different from the company’s main financial targets, showing that the street is optimistic that Zurich can execute its business strategy well and deliver on its financial targets.

This also means that Zurich’s dividend should continue a growing path, like it has delivered over the past few years. Its last annual dividend was CHF 28 per share, up by 7.7% compared to the previous year. Its dividend payout ratio was 70%, which is not a conservative level compared to other companies in the sector, but it’s an acceptable ratio in my opinion considering its defensive business profile and excess capitalization over the past few years. As shown in the next graph, Zurich’s solvency ratio has consistently been above its minimum target level over the last decade, even during 2020 when its earnings and capital were hit by Covid-19 and increased capital markets volatility.

Going forward, its dividend payout ratio should remain at relatively high levels, as the company doesn’t need to retain earnings due to its strong capitalization. This means its dividend growth should come mainly from earnings growth, probably leading to annual dividend increase at high single digits. According to analysts’ estimates, its dividend per share should be about CHF 29 related to 2025 earnings, up by 3.9% YoY, which seems quite conservative and Zurich seems to have room to beat current expectations. At its current share price, this leads to a forward dividend yield of about 5.2%, which seems to be attractive to income-oriented investors.

While Zurich has a very good capital position and could be more aggressive regarding its capital returns, investors should be aware that insurance companies are usually quite conservative about the use of their ‘excess’ capital, especially in Europe. Zurich is no exception and has made relatively small buybacks in the past, while it’s not currently performing a share buyback program. Indeed, its last share buyback was completed in 2024 for a total amount of CHF 1.1 billion, or 1.5% of its market value at the time, and over the last year Zurich has not repurchased its own shares. I think this is also a reason why its share price has traded sideways in recent months, as the company’s capital return strategy is quite conservative, while other companies in the financial sector have been much more aggressive lately on returning capital to shareholders.

Regarding its valuation, Zurich is currently trading at about 4x book value (vs. 3.9x book value when I last analyzed it), which remains a very high valuation. As can be seen in the next graph, Zurich’s current P/BV multiple is at the top of its historical valuation over the last decade, which seems to be a sign of overvaluation.

In my opinion, I don’t see much reason for a higher multiple ahead, as the company is not growing that much and profitability is not expected to improve compared to its recent levels, as Zurich’s own target is to have a lower ROE ahead compared to what it has achieved in recent quarters. Moreover, compared to peers this is also a premium valuation, as other European insurance companies such as AXA (OTCQX:AXAHY), Allianz, or Generali (OTCPK:ARZGY) are all trading at lower valuation multiples than Zurich.

Conclusion

Zurich has delivered a positive operating performance in recent quarters, but growth prospects ahead are somewhat muted and its main financial targets over the next couple of years don’t appear to be very ambitious. While Zurich offers a relatively high and sustainable dividend yield, its premium valuation doesn’t leave much room for upside potential and the company’s conservative capital return policy also doesn’t help to drive a higher share price ahead. Therefore, I maintain my ‘Hold’ recommendation and see income as the most attractive feature of its investment case.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bitcoin Depot: Stellar Q3, But Soft Near-Term Outlook Dampens Momentum
data.description:
In the past week, virtually every stock with Bitcoin (BTC-USD) in its name has sold off in an equal response to Bitcoin’s own drawdown. Bitcoin Depot (BTM) is down ~70% from the yearly high attained in June.

Q3 results were released last week amidst the weak sentiment in the crypto market. Near-term forward-looking guidance was softer than expected for Bitcoin Depot (between $112 million and $115 million revenue in Q4), and this in turn has made investors cautious despite the growing revenue and the company making some solid strides in operations in Q3 and for the better part of the year. A recent $15 million equity raise through the sale of 4,285,716 class A shares at $3.50 per share last month, which increased public float, has all added downward pressure on BTM share price.

In the Q3 results, Bitcoin Depot delivered strong revenue growth, maintained a positive bottom line, and grew cash reserves. Revenue grew by 20% YoY to reach $162.5 million. Gross profit was $28.2 million, and net income was $5.5 million, or $0.08 per share. Gross margin was 17.4%, an improvement of 250 basis points compared to a year ago (it was 14.9% gross margin in Q3 2024). The 250 bps gross margin improvement over a 20% revenue growth shows that Bitcoin Depot is effectively increasing the profit they keep from each transaction while scaling up volume. Adjusted EBITDA improved by 75% YoY to $16.1 million.

Operationally, Bitcoin Depot scaled its active kiosk fleet to ~9,300 machines while seeing robust consumer demand push the median transaction size up 40% YoY to $350, which ultimately catapulted revenue to $162.5 million. International expansion also took more shape in Q3, majorly in Australia. And locally, the acquisition of National Bitcoin ATM assets added over 500 kiosks and expanded Bitcoin Depot's reach across 27 states. That acquisition is a major highlight because it means Bitcoin Depot absorbed one of its major competitors and has secured a broader revenue base.

Liquidity was solidified in Q3 with $59.3 million in cash and equivalents, and $13.6 million in working capital and crypto treasury assets, bringing total liquidity to $72.9 million. CapEx was high in Q3 at $4.1 million, but operations were also highly efficient, and cash from operations of $6.6 million offset more than the CapEx, and Bitcoin Depot ended the quarter with $2.55 million in free cash flow (based on an unadjusted calculation that differs from Bitcoin Depot's reported $6.5 million FCF). $8.8 million was also raised through financing activities in Q3. It seems Bitcoin Depot is aggressively pursuing both debt and equity financing to capture market share through both organic kiosk expansion and strategic acquisitions, like in the case of the National Bitcoin acquisition. I think the raises (like the $15 million raised last month, mentioned in the opening of the piece) serve to replenish liquidity after the acquisition and other investments, and to provide an ample runway to navigate the expected regulatory headwinds and revenue drop in Q4.

Total debt now stands at $72.15 million, but isn't an overhang yet, as the interest coverage ratio is around 2.8x. Interest expense for Q3 was $4.1 million and was covered well by the $11.8 million operating income, with $5.5 million left as net profit.

Management mentioned some regulatory headwinds in the Q3 earnings call and expected QoQ revenue decline in Q4 due to seasonality. Softer Q4 revenue guidance due to seasonality does not come as a surprise. For investors familiar with Bitcoin Depot, Q4 has always turned out to be the typically weakest fiscal quarter for the company’s top line compared to Q2 and Q3. This might cloud Bitcoin Depot's short-term prospects, but the full integration of the acquired National Bitcoin assets (which include over 500 Bitcoin ATMs) by the end of Q4 will be a positive offset going into 2026.

​Management also mentioned some regulatory headwinds going into Q4 which include stricter KYC requirements and caps on transactions. This isn't the first time caps on transactions at Bitcoin ATMs have been introduced by regulators. Bitcoin Depot has navigated these regulatory headwinds in the past. For instance, early last year when regulators in California (and some other states followed suit) began operating under the Senate Bill SB 401 which was passed and imposed stricter regulatory compliance protocols for KYC and AML and a cap on daily transactions at Bitcoin ATMs within the jurisdiction to $1,000 a day, that was a low point for the business which caused a stretch of revenue decline last year, but Bitcoin Depot has in turn ramped up compliance efforts and offered customers a workaround by combining the Bitcoin ATM Service with BDCheckout to effectively increase the daily limit, the evidence is in the recovery of revenue this year and the gross margin improvement on transaction volumes.

In early October, we announced the rollout of our new compliance standards that make Bitcoin Depot one of the only operators in the industry to require customer identification before transacting for any amount of money. This initiative applies to all new and existing customers, ensuring they benefit from the highest level of protection well beyond what is currently required by federal law. - CEO Brandon Mintz, during the Q3 earnings call.

In light of emerging compliance challenges, management in the Q3 call reiterated Bitcoin Depot's commitment to being regulatory complaint and pointed to recent rollout of mandatory KYC verification by Bitcoin Depot to keep pace with ongoing regulatory requirements. They even saw this as an opportunity where smaller, non-compliant Bitcoin ATM operators will be forced out of the market, which opens more market opportunities for bigger, compliant operators like Bitcoin Depot. The only caveat I see here is that as regulatory requirements tighten, SG&A costs could also potentially rise because increased monitoring and compliance staffing, and legal support are necessary to manage the more complex legal and regulatory frameworks.

With the drop from June’s high, Bitcoin Depot is now one of the few crypto stocks that trade at attractive sales and earnings multiples. P/S ratio is currently 0.09x, and the forward P/S is 0.2x, while the forward P/E is 4.51x. It is a bit rare these days to get these lower-end valuation multiples for a crypto company. But Bitcoin Depot's business model is one the broader market has not fully recognized over the years, compared to interest in Bitcoin miners and even the more corporate digital asset treasury companies. While valuation looks attractive, with softer guidance and potential regulatory headwinds in the near term, caution is still warranted.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Capex Spending On AI Is Masking Economic Weakness
data.description:
The U.S. economy’s recent growth has a distinctive engine: large‑scale capital expenditures (capex) tied to artificial intelligence (AI). Firms such as Microsoft (MSFT), Alphabet (Google) (GOOG)(GOOGL), Meta Platforms (META), and Amazon (AMZN) have announced massive investments in data centers, servers, networking equipment, and AI infrastructure. As noted by Investing.com:

“Artificial intelligence is consuming capital faster than investors can recalibrate. Bank of America now sees global hyperscale spending rising 67% in 2025 and another 31% in 2026, with total outlays climbing to $611 billion. That is a $145 billion increase in just one month’s estimates.

The surge shows how cloud giants are doubling down. Google raised its 2025 capital budget to $92 billion, Microsoft plans even faster growth into fiscal 2026, and Meta now expects spending of about $100 billion in 2026. Amazon’s data center capacity is on track to double by 2027. None show intent to slow down, even as capex intensity approaches 30% of sales, roughly triple historic norms.

That level of investment is extraordinary. At its peak, the 5G telecom buildout consumed about 70% of operating cash flow, AI infrastructure is now approaching the same strain.

While we can certainly discuss the magnitude of those investments and the risks associated with repeating another “Dot.com” overbuild, the point I want to address with you today is how those capital expenditures are masking broader economic weakness.

For example, a recent estimate places U.S. AI-related capex for fiscal 2025 at about 1.2% of GDP. (The chart below uses the Atlanta Fed GDPNow estimate for Q3 of 4% nominal GDP growth and assumes the same in Q4.) If we subtract out the AI-related capex spending, growth is significantly weaker than advertised.

In raw terms, the global AI investment by key players already exceeds hundreds of billions of dollars. Analysts forecast global AI spend at around US $360 billion in 2025 with growth into 2026 and beyond. For instance, data center capex is projected to grow at a 21% CAGR to reach US$1.2 trillion globally by 2029. Such figures highlight real spending momentum, and that momentum has helped the U.S. economy avoid a steeper decline in growth. But this growth is highly concentrated. Only a handful of large tech firms comprise the bulk of the capex. Therefore, the headline numbers require deeper interpretation. Investors must recognize that, while the impact on economic growth is real, spending will eventually slow down.

Still, the rise of AI-driven investment is significant for the economy and for investors alike. It signals a shift in the composition of growth from consumption and broad business investment toward heavy-asset, tech-centric investment. Recognizing how that shift works is critical for understanding risks and opportunities.

What the Boom Masks – Underlying Weakness in the Economy

Although the surge in AI investment is impressive, it masks several structural weaknesses in the broader U.S. economy. First, the AI capex boom is concentrated among a small number of firms and sectors rather than being broadly diffused across all industries or geographies. The bulk of spending is going into servers, data centers, and networks. While those assets are capital-intensive, they are not labor-intensive in the way large manufacturing or services growth might be. As noted above, while analysts estimate that AI capex may be 1.2% of GDP in 2025 under a standard multiplier, the real economic benefits in productivity or employment outside of the tech sector remain limited so far. We observe that in the dispersion of expected 2026 earnings growth between the largest market capitalization-weighted stocks in the S&P 500 index and the rest.

In other words, if AI capex spending reached a broad swath of the economy, the earnings expectations for the bottom 493 companies would not be negative. It is also crucial to note that forward earnings estimates are always overly optimistic, so the results are likely to be worse in the future.

Second, much of that investment relies on imported equipment, components, and technologies, which means the domestic multiplier of the spending is weaker than the headline number suggests. Although AI capex is large, much of it is still classified as intermediate goods, which aren’t fully captured in GDP statistics. However, while AI capex spending is robust, spending by the rest of the economy remains muted.

Third, when you look beyond the tech sector, the traditional engine blocks of growth are weaker. Residential investment is under pressure as housing affordability remains an issue. As noted above, since business investment outside the large tech players remains muted, that is weighing on employment growth, which continues to show signs of softening.

Fourth, while AI capital expenditures (capex) are high, the economic payoff has not yet been fully proven. Productivity gains, revenue gains, and sustainable earnings from this wave of infrastructure spend have not been fully realized. One Vanguard analysis notes that to move U.S. growth above trend via AI alone would require approximately US$1 trillion in AI-related spending, which lies ahead, not behind.

Thus, the underlying condition of the economy is more fragile than the capital spend numbers imply. The risk is that when the tech capex boom slows or fails to deliver a broad spillover, the rest of the economy will feel the weakness more sharply.

Therefore, as an investor, the risk of assuming broad-based resilience may be critical to consider when developing your investment thesis.

Implications for Investors

For investors, the mixed nature of this growth wave presents both opportunity and risk. The current opportunity for investors is to invest directly in firms closely tied to AI infrastructure, such as chip manufacturers, data center operators, and cloud services companies, all of which are likely to benefit. Their growth trajectories may outpace the broader economy because they are at the heart of the capital expenditure surge. But these opportunities come with important caveats.

One risk is concentration. If a narrow subset of companies or sectors is driving the growth story, then portfolios that lack diversification towards non-tech may expose investors to sharper corrections. If the tech capex wave slows, valuations tied to presumed growth may reverse quickly, especially among firms with aggressive capital expenditures and uncertain near-term returns. For example, analysts at Goldman Sachs warn that the current contributions of AI to GDP are likely understated; however, the actual economic benefit remains modest, and future risks remain high.

Secondly, as we saw during the dot-com bubble, not all companies that jumped into the internet market survived. Those failures also included some of the largest companies at the time, such as Enron, World.com, and Lucent, among others. The current AI cycle will likely be the same; there will be some big long-term winners, but there will also be quite a few companies that are mainly trading on “hope” for future results that are far from guaranteed.

Another investor implication concerns earnings quality. Heavy capital expenditures do not guarantee near-term earnings improvement or productivity gains. Some firms may carry high depreciation, amortization, and idle capacity risk. A report notes that capital spending growth now may generate returns only years down the road. This remains one of our primary concerns, as expectations for future earnings growth are incredibly elevated. This leaves an enormous amount of room for disappointment when combined with already high valuation multiples, making the downside risk not inconsequential.

(The chart shows the current deviation of earnings growth from its long-term exponential growth trend versus the trailing P/E ratio, which is inverted. When the “E” reverses, valuations will skyrocket as they did during the Dot.com bust, the Financial crisis, and the Pandemic shutdown.)

Third, investors should monitor the masking effect. The fact that AI capex is propping up headline growth means the rest of the economy remains vulnerable. As shown, the economically weighted ISM index (70% services/30% manufacturing) remains in expansion territory, but just barely. If consumption or non-tech business investment falters, the broader weakness may surface suddenly. Portfolios built only around tech optimism may lack cushions from areas less tied to the boom.

Fourth, valuations need discipline. As noted above, investors are currently pricing in the most optimistic of future outcomes. That exponentially increases the risk of disappointment at some point in the future. The correction potential rises if growth disappoints, returns are delayed, or macro weakness intensifies. Investors should consider whether the current growth base is sufficiently broad to support the expected outcomes. Are earnings projections realistic? How much is the stock’s valuation already assuming perfect execution?

In short, you must not assume that because one part of the economy is booming, everything else is strong. Please recognize that the growth narrative is narrow; therefore, as investors, we should consider some practical steps to manage future risks.

Thoughts About Investing In This Cycle

The AI investment boom is real and meaningful, and it does support parts of the U.S. economy and investors’ portfolios, at least for today. Yet it is not a substitute for broad-based economic strength. The concentration of growth, reliance on imports, muted spillover into employment and productivity, and unproven near-term returns all signal caution.

For investors, this means a focus on risk rather than complacency. A portfolio overly reliant on the tech apex narrative may be exposed if the broader economy weakens or if the capex wave slows. A strategy grounded in diversification, earnings discipline, risk monitoring, and awareness of structural fragility may help navigate the uneven growth landscape. In this environment, you must find opportunities, but beware the hidden weakness behind the bright numbers.

Here are seven practical steps to help manage the risk:

  1. Trim exposure to high-valuation tech stocks. Reduce overweight positions in companies where valuations depend on continued, aggressive AI-driven capital expenditure growth.
  2. Diversify beyond the AI supply chain. Broaden your portfolio to include sectors less reliant on AI momentum. Think utilities, healthcare, and consumer staples.
  3. Focus on free cash flow and earnings stability. Prioritize companies generating strong operating cash flow with proven earnings, not firms betting on long-run AI returns.
  4. Increase allocations to short-duration fixed income. In a weakening macroeconomic environment, shorter-duration bonds reduce interest rate risk and provide greater flexibility.
  5. Watch real economy indicators closely. Track labor market data, consumer sentiment, and non‑tech business investment for signs of broad economic direction.
  6. Use hedges for concentrated risk. If exposure to large‑cap tech is unavoidable, consider options strategies or inverse funds to manage downside.
  7. Avoid chasing the AI narrative. Wait for proof of productivity gains and margin expansion before adding to AI-exposed positions. Patience beats FOMO.

Stay disciplined. A narrow growth story has historically been a risky proposition. Instead, position for strength that’s durable and not driven by a single theme.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Rocket Lab: The Neutron Delay Is Not A Major Hit
data.description:
In my last coverage of Rocket Lab Corporation (RKLB), I upgraded my rating to a Buy, citing the upcoming launch of the Neutron as a nice catalyst. I said:

The first launch is key, as so far, there are only 3 Neutron missions currently in backlog. According to CEO Peter Beck, commercial customers "want to see a rocket that works before they commit."

This first launch is key to the approval of the Part 450 license. Without it, the company is not able to launch a Neutron rocket from U.S. soil at Wallops Island, VA, for any customer (including the $515M deal with the Space Development Agency).

Despite the recent Neutron schedule slippage, with management pushing the first platform arrival at LC-3 to Q1 2026, I remain bullish on the stock.

Below, I explain in detail why.

Q3 2025 Results

Rocket Lab reported a clean top line and margin improvement, with revenue up 48% YOY and GAAP gross margin 37% (vs. 26.7% in Q3 last year). That said, the company is still burning cash, with an accelerated operating loss of $59M, compared to $52M in Q3 2024.

From a mix perspective, the product segment is still the dominant in the mix, with $104M reported in Q3 (up 31% YOY). In my view, the improvement in gross margins was mainly driven by the outperformance of the services segment, which reported a top-line growth of 101% YOY.

A quick look at the cash flow statement shows operating cash outflows of $101M for the 9M 2025 period. Furthermore, CapEx was $107M.

I'm not particularly concerned about this cash burn. Why?

Well, the company exited the quarter "with $1+ billion" in liquidity after the recent ATM offering program. It seems that dilution is not putting off the market (based on price action since the start of the year), so I'm not sweating too much about this negative factor. I will discuss what indeed makes me sweat in the risk section. But before that, let me explain why I'm bullish on Rocket Lab.

Why I'm Still Bullish

The main pillar of my bull case is still the impressive cadence of the Electron platform. In the last quarter alone, the company closed 17 Electron contracts, and management reiterated over 20 launches in 2025. In fact, since the last print, the company launched a HASTE on November 18 and announced a second Electron launch just 24h later. That's two launches in two days, from two hemispheres (Virginia and New Zealand, respectively).

Overall, I feel quite confident about the $170-$180M guidance for Q4, which represents 32% YOY growth.

Speaking of growth, as long as revenue is growing above 30%, I am comfortable with my Buy rating. As a side note, the company has been growing its top line above 30% in each quarter since Q3 2023.

My other pillar is the combination of space systems and the defense momentum from HASTE as a secondary engine. In Q3, there were two back-to-back HASTE missions, which, coupled with the one I mentioned above in Q4, validate a repeatable test platform. On the bottom line, I see upside from the recent outperformance of the systems segment. Management guided Q4 non-GAAP gross margins in the 43% to 45% range.

Finally, the last pillar is the Neutron platform. The Street is expecting an acceleration in growth in the back half of 2026 and throughout 2027.

I believe that growth is mainly driven by the Neutron platform. That said, there are a few risks that I wanted to highlight, especially after the recent delay of this rocket.

Risks

The main downside that I see for my bull case is the Neutron schedule slippage. Management pushed hardware arrival at LC-3 to Q1 2026 with the first launch thereafter, contingent on qualification. This defers revenue and potential gross margin step-ups into H2 2026, rather than the initial expectations for late 2025.

I argue that the optimism around this stock didn't take a major hit due to this delay, as price action right after earnings was moderately positive. In fact, the stock rallied 7.8% in the first hour after announcing earnings.

I believe the selloff thereafter was mainly driven by the high beta of this stock. According to Seeking Alpha, the 24-month beta of this stock is 2.15. Considering the selloff in the broader markets, despite Nvidia's beat and a positive September payroll report, the pessimism didn't turn around as the chance for another rate cut in the December FOMC meeting has decreased significantly over the last few weeks (especially after the BLS delayed the November jobs report to December 16, almost a week after the FOMC meeting).

Back to Rocket Lab, this means that price action will be pressured in the next weeks due to the high beta of this stock. From a price action perspective, the price is right at a historical support level. If we break below $40 (on volume), the next stop could be at $30.

Going into the back end of this year and Q1 2026, I would keep a close eye on three main factors. I need to see positive signs of vehicle arrival at LC-3 by the end of March. Once the vehicle has arrived, there will be a qualification phase before the launch. Finally, I need clarity on the first mission profile. Any slips could lead to the Street to haircut the medium-lift contribution from their 2026 estimates and lean harder on Electron/Space Systems. So far, over the last 4 weeks, the Street has been trimming its top-line projections for H2 next year (likely due to the Neutron delay).

If this downward revision trend continues due to further delays over the next two quarters, I may reconsider my Buy rating.

Conclusion

Overall, I believe Rocket Lab stock remains a Buy. I see a strong cadence in the Electron program, with growing space systems revenue in the triple digits, which is accretive to margins. The consolidated top-line growth remains above 30%, which, in my view, justifies the premium that investors are paying today for shares.

Let me be clear, paying today 35x for next year's sales is not what I would consider a deep value play. In fact, that multiple is 20x the industrials sector median. However, Rocket Lab is not a value stock. This is a growth stock, with several catalysts ahead (the main one being the Neutron platform and the $0.5B SDA program) that could reignite growth from the low 30s% to the mid 50s% by H1 2027. As long as Neutron remains on track for Q1 2026, and the qualification tests are positive prior to the first launch next year, I remain bullish on this stock. That said, any slippage could lead the Street to revise downwards their top-line estimates.

data.title: Oaktree Specialty Lending: The Yield Trap Is Still Active, Downgrade
data.description:
Oaktree Specialty Lending Corporation (OCSL) has been one of those BDCs, which has multiple times fallen into an optically attractive discount territory, but still somehow managed to disappoint the "buy the dip" investors.

For example, in August 2024, I issued a bearish thesis on this BDC, where since then the share price has dropped down by ~19%. After the release of the next financial report, OCSL's share price declined, offering investors a hefty discount and elevated yield. However, by assessing the situation more deeply, it was clear to me that there would be not only steep NAV corrections going forward, but also a material dividend cut to emerge from the deeply underfunded distribution situation. My case worked out almost perfectly - i.e., OCSL announced a dividend cut and experienced notable NAV erosion.

Finally, as we entered 2025, OCSL was trading also at a much deeper discount than its peers (0.87x for OCSL vs. ~0.95x for the sector median). Because of the recent dividend cut, there was a significant load of margin of safety embedded in OCSL's business - e.g., base dividend coverage of 136%. The leverage, due to external equity injection by Oaktree, stood at a quite favorable level as well - at around 1.0x, which is indicative of sufficient firepower to expand the portfolio. However, I decided to assume a skeptical stance and avoid OCSL, primarily, because of my fears surrounding the non-accrual base, which was really the main catalyst for OCSL's struggles (it is something that we cannot directly measure, but simply have to judge based on the past underwriting decisions). Again, since then OCSL has lost an additional 14% of its market cap.

Having said that, recently, OCSL released its Q4, 2025 earnings report, which initially, when it came out, caused the market to react positively. As the trading day progressed, the gains abated, but still, compared to the BDC index (BIZD), OCSL finished materially higher.

So, the question is whether now is the time to finally invest in OCSL?

Thesis review

Currently, OCSL trades at P/NAV of 0.82x and offers a yield of 12.01%. The discount is indeed material on an absolute basis, but relative to the sector median of 0.81x, we could even say that OCSL trades at a slight premium.

For example, OCSL is very similarly priced to such quality BDC names as Morgan Stanley Direct Lending Fund (MSDL), Barings BDC (BBDC) and PennantPark Floating Rate Capital (PFLT). Yet, what I have to immediately say is that none of these three players have experienced anything that would even come close to OCSL's turbulences with non-accruals.

What it implies is that there has to be something special, something that is above-average in order to justify the fact that OCSL does not trade at a deeper discount area like, for example, FSK KKR Capital (FSK) - i.e., P/NAV of 0.71x and also troubles with non-accruals.

I will start with the good and then transition to the bad and the ugly.

OCSL ended the most recent quarter with a debt-to-equity ratio of 1.02x, which means that it has the necessary balance sheet capacity to drive top-line growth. However, already here I have to caveat that most likely all of this growth will be offset with the lagged effects from the previous base rate cut and, even more importantly, from those that will happen in either late 2025 or early 2026.

In Q4, 2025, it was also positive to see that now all of OCSL's leverage is tied to floating rates, meaning that there are no outstanding unfavorable below market level yield notes left. Namely, I see no material refinancing risk that would impose additional headwinds to OCSL's NII per share generation.

Also, OCSL has done a decent cleaning of its portfolio by solving a large part of its non-accruals and doing relevant debt investment markdowns to prepare for a more stable path going forward. This was the main driver for OCSL's NAV declining from the previous quarter (by less than 1%).

This is where the positive story ends.

About the bad.

OCSL's base dividend coverage has again dropped to the absolute minimum of 102.5%. The adjusted NII per share came in at $0.41, which is higher than in the previous quarter (due to many negative one-offs affecting Q3), but way below Q2 2025, when the adjusted NII per share figure was $0.45

The combination of thin dividend coverage and a stagnant NII per share path does not bode well for OCSL, especially given the fact that in the recent past OCSL has struggled a lot with unexpected non-accruals. In other words, there is no margin of safety for OCSL to absorb any potential shocks.

Again, the only realistic way for OCSL to secure NII per share growth is through balance sheet expansion, as there is no option to retain internal equity from undistributed NII nor is there an option to issue fresh equity to fund new loan investments. Yet, keeping in mind that in the next quarter there will be pressures from lower base rates and continued yield compression (as portfolio in-place yields remain above what OCSL can source in the market now), I do not see how OCSL could emerge from this "walking on thin ice" moment.

About the ugly.

The key risk of OCSL that would for sure keep me awake at night if I deployed my precious capital here is the significant exposure to software companies:

It would be hard to find another first-lien-focused BDC with so considerable exposure to the software & services segment. In my view, this is where the disruptive AI risks lie. Now, if we couple this with the fact that in Q4 2025, OCSL's median portfolio company median EBITDA declined by ~7% and leverage increased by 0.01x, I think that it would be fair to say that we should assign at least some discount to account for potential non-accruals that might stem from here.

The bottom line

To summarize, I have to say that OCSL's yield trap, about which I previously warned, has only partially worked. Namely, the trap is still active and risky for yield-seeking investors.

I would not be surprised if in H1 2026 we saw yet another dividend cut announced by OCSL. There are two high probability drivers for this: 1) more interest rate cuts by the Fed that would put additional pressure on the already exhausted dividend coverage level and/or 2) unexpected non-accruals that could automatically result in steep declines of NII per share.

I would suggest investors avoid OCSL until we get offered a tangible discount to its closest first-lien BDC peers.

data.title: Buy This Dip In Advance Of A Year-End Rally
data.description:
The bull was back at yesterday’s open for the first hour of trade until being summarily dismissed by a wave of selling that started in the cryptocurrency space and spread like wildfire through the rest of the market. The 1.4% gain for the S&P 500 index at the open turned into a 1.6% loss by day’s end in the largest intraday swing since the market bottomed in a tariff tantrum last April. While the bears seem invigorated, I don’t think the swing reflected disappointment in what were stellar results from Nvidia. Instead, it looked more like the massive unwinding of leveraged bets on crypto, led by Bitcoin, which began its descent in mid-October from $125,000. That may continue today until the leverage clears, but bears will use it to argue the economy is in trouble and the bull market may be at risk, as many have been doing unsuccessfully for the past three years.

It is no coincidence that the technology sector and many of the most expensive momentum growth stocks were the hardest hit yesterday, as well as over the past three weeks. The same lot that is levered in the crypto space, predominantly retail investors, is likely levered in these names as well. Therefore, when Bitcoin suffers a bear market decline of 30%, it forces the unwinding of leverage in everything else these speculative investors own. Of course, the selling has been compounded by fears of overvaluation in the tech sector and concerns about a bubble in AI. Once this clears, like a nasty case of the flu, investors should refocus on the economy, which continues to defy the naysayers.

Despite a trade policy that has upended the normal course of business for the past seven months, the economy continues to rebound from its first half slump. The Atlanta Fed’s GDPNow forecaster sees growth in the third quarter running at 4.2%, led by a 3.4% growth rate in consumer spending, which accounts for more than two-thirds of our economic output. Consumers have been saying one thing with sentiment at all-time lows, while doing others, as in spending on goods and services. While we have not had the government’s data the past six weeks, I have noted that the private sector’s Redbook Same-Store Sales Index continues to report strong sales growth, as we saw in this week’s annualized growth rate of 6.1%. That was the strongest in four months. The PMI reports for October and November indicated softer but still above trend growth rates for the fourth quarter.

Yesterday’s employment data for September was encouraging, as the economy added 119,000 jobs, but the prior two months saw downward revisions of 33,000. The sectors creating jobs narrowed to just healthcare and leisure and hospitality, while the unemployment rate edged up to 4.4%. Wage growth held steady at 3.8%, which means that workers are still realizing real wage growth, as the rate of inflation hovers close to 3%. That is the key to real consumer spending growth. This is dated information, and I suspect October’s report will be a grimmer picture, but weekly unemployment claims are not rising meaningfully. This is still a limited hire and limited fire labor market, but it’s a wake-up call to the Fed to act in December. I still expect a rate cut at the December 10 meeting.

The S&P 500 has now officially completed a pullback of 5.1%. The Nasdaq Composite surrendered 7.3%, and the Nasdaq 100 (QQQ) fell 7.4%. Lastly, the Russell 2000 index (IWM) is down 8.5% from its all-time high. I see this as nothing but a positive reset from which to start a year-end rally characterized by sector rotation from technology into more value-oriented sectors and stocks in the market. Some of yesterday’s relative outperformers give a good indication of where the money is going. I think investors should be buying this dip in anticipation of a year-end rally and more bull market gains in 2026.

A bottom in Bitcoin may coincide with the lows for the major market indexes. This bellwether is down 30% from its highs and approaching its April low around 80,000. I think that should serve as support.

This fear is palpable, as CNN’s Fear & Greed Index shows a reading of 6! The headlines are riddled with negative news, and few are suggesting that a bottom is close. That is a perfect setup for a turnback.

Note that this index has nearly matched the abysmal level it reached during the height of the tariff tantrum in early April. That low came close to the bottom for the S&P 500, and the beginning of a ferocious rally.

I don’t see a repeat of that recovery, but I do see a rebound that is broadly based in which investors start to follow the improvement in breadth for earnings growth. There are countless stocks trading at half the multiple of the S&P 500 index with very attractive growth prospects for 2026. This is a very much needed pullback or correction, depending on the index, that should serve to expel the market of the speculative excesses built up over the past three years.
